title: About
text:
About this site
“Rainy Algorithms” is a metaphorical term that combines “rainy” from your description of valuing rain and “algorithms” from the coding and optimization aspects mentioned in your blog’s content. It symbolizes the idea of combining meteorology (rain-related topics) with coding and optimization (algorithm-related topics). Just as rain can bring transformation and change, “Rainy Algorithms” suggests a fusion of elements that can bring about meaningful insights and transformations in atmospheric science through coding and optimization techniques.

title: General Assessment
text:
Remarks:

Use any programming languages you would like to. Keep in mind that it should be a language capable of handling scientific computations. We, generally, are familiar with R and Python.
The Internet is filled with Q&A regarding programming, and there is definitely someone who tried to program what you want to do. So, Google is your friend.
Please submit your code and results in a readable manner.
Comment your code, and leave notes, therefore, you or other people can read it easily later.


Task 1: String manipulation
Write a code that can do these tasks:

Print "Hello World!".
Check out your working directory (WD) and Print it.
Make a new directory from within your code.
Change your WD to the newly created directory.
Create a TXT file in the WD, and Write in it:
"Hello world!"
"I’m your name"
Read that text file and replace "World" with "Dresden" or any other word, and finally write it to the TXT file.
Advanced task: If you want to challenge yourself, this is the chance:

Create a vector of string that has a length of 50 elements. Each element is, for example, "Hello! I’m file number n". n is the order of the string in the vector.
Loop over the vector elements, and write each element to a TXT file called "file_n.txt"
List all these TXT files and read their content to one variable, and write this variable to a TXT file called "all_n.txt"
Delete the other 50 files.




Task 2: One Dimensional Data
Go to Climate Change Knowledge Portal. Download a time series data of precipitation and mean temperature for your country or area of interest. After downloading the data as shown in Figure 1, continue in the same code you started in Task 1 and write a script that:



Climate Change Knowledge Portal. CRU (Observed)



Read the two CSV file you downloaded correctly.
Plot a time series of the two variables.
Produce a long-term climatology from the data. Look at the first left plot here.
Advanced task: If you want to challenge yourself, this is another chance:

calculate the yearly accumulated rainfall and plot it as bar-plot where x-axis is years.
add a trend line with slope value on the chart.




Task 3: Two Dimensional Data
Go to CloudStore, TU Dresden and download the file task3_file.nc. The file contains 2D data (excluding time dimension) from ERA5 reanalysis including 2 m temperature, total precipitation, u and v wind compounds. After downloading the file, continue in the same code you started in tasks above and write a script that:

Read the netCDF file.
Extract different variables from the file.
Determine the spatial resolution of data in longitude and latitude directions.
search for the grid cell that represents your region of interest, and extract the variables as monthly time series.
Plot 2 m temperature, total precipitation in one time series plot (Hint: two different y-axis).
Plot the u and v wind components as well as wind speed.
Advance task: If you want to challenge yourself, this is another chance:

Aggregate the variables on the time axis and produce a long term climatology.
Plot a map focused on your region showing long-term climatology of 2 m temperature, total precipitation, and wind. (Hint: the data has dimension of (time=264, lat=180, lon=360), a worldwide climatology should have (time=1, lat=180, lon=360)).
Try to add map legends, shape files indicating the borders and every features that is part of map standards.
You could plot wind speed and directions as arrows.




Task 4: Three Dimensional Data (Advanced)
Go to CloudStore, TU Dresden and download the file task4_file.nc. The file contains 3D data (excluding time dimension) from ERA5 reanalysis including temperature, relative humidity, geopotential (See more about it here), and u and v wind compounds. After downloading the file, continue in the same code you started in tasks above and write a script that:

Read the netCDF file.
Extract different variables from the file.
Determine the spatial resolution of data in longitude and latitude directions.
Search for the grid cell that represents your region of interest, and extract the variables as monthly time series. (Hint: the file contains atmospheric data on variable levels for one year. So the monthly time series should a table for the location you picked containing 3 columns: month, level, value)
Plot atmospheric profiles for different variable, where x-axis is the climatic variable, y-axis is pressure level or/and altitude. Start with plotting one month. Check the first figure on the right there.
Plot similar profile the u and v wind components as well as wind speed.
Advanced task: If you want to challenge yourself, this is another chance:

In the plots above, add a profile line for each month for each variable.



End

title: Research Topics
text:
I wish to express complete transparency in our collaboration expectations. The acquisition of programming skills is imperative for us to work together effectively. Our endeavors encompass the management of extensive datasets and intricate simulations. Programming serves as a fundamental tool for achieving automation and optimizing efficiency within these contexts. To facilitate a successful partnership, I kindly request your commitment to acquiring programming proficiency. This will not only enhance our productivity but also contribute to the overall success of our collaboration. Your understanding and consideration in this matter are greatly appreciated.

Analysis of CAPE and CIN from radiosondes for Germany
Supervisors: K. Barfus1 & A. Homoudi2 (Chair of Meteorology)
1 klemens.barfus@tu-dresden.de 2 ahmed.homoudi@tu-dresden.de
Vertical profiles of the atmosphere are measured using Radiosondes/ weather balloons. These measurements provide some insights if thunderstorms might happen by indices like CAPE and CIN. The project aims to fullfil the following objectives: - Relevant literature review. - Estimation of CAPE, CIN, & other atmospheric stability indices. - Systematic analysis and presentation of the results.

Identification of Tropical cyclones in the Arabian Sea Sub-Basin
Supervisors: A. Homoudi1 & K. Barfus2 (Chair of Meteorology)
1 ahmed.homoudi@tu-dresden.de
2 klemens.barfus@tu-dresden.de
Background:
Tropical cyclones (Tcs) are one of the most devastating events and are expected to intensify under global warming. TCs are one of the most difficult events to predict and understand in the atmosphere. Storm surges associated with severe TCs are the most damaging rather than rain and strong winds. Under a warming climate, the frequency of the TCs in the Arabian Sea is increasing. Additionally, the TCs are projected to intensify, with a greater proportion of storms having more damaging wind speeds, higher storm surges, and more extreme rainfall rates. Besides the fact that TCs are spatially shifted in Climate models, the detection criteria vary tremendously between climate models and remote sensing data. Therefore, the characteristics of TCs should be understood through direct, objective and automatic detection and tracking methods in reanalyses and model simulations to contribute to developing integrated planning for Climate Change.
Objectives:

Detecting tropical cyclones genesis from Remote sensing data (e.g., IR images)
Identification of tropical cyclones in ERA5 reanalysis data.
Comparison of the findings with observation data (i.e. IBTrACS)
Systematic presentation of findings

Skills (not compulsory but preferred or strong motivation to learn):

Programming with R or Python
Familiarity with Linux and shell scripting
Acquaintance with climate data formats (e.g., NetCDF)



Climatology of the Moisture Sources in the Arabian Peninsula using Lagrangian Method
Supervisors: A. Homoudi1 & K. Barfus2 (Chair of Meteorology)
1 ahmed.homoudi@tu-dresden.de
2 klemens.barfus@tu-dresden.de
Background:
The Arabian Peninsula is one of the aridest regions on earth, and it is a region of reciprocal influences of tropical and extra-tropical configurations. Several configurations govern the climate of the region (e.g., Mediterranean, monsoon, subtropical, tropical, and Saharan influences). It is important to understand the configuration contributing to precipitation events in such an arid region. One essential ingredient for precipitation events is the available moisture, which can, e.g. be transported in organised bands known as atmospheric rivers. These different means of moisture transport are responsible for some of the global hydro-climatic extremes. In the Arabian Peninsula, they are responsible for the record floods during March 2019. On the other hand, these extreme precipitation events might be the only source of groundwater recharge.
Objectives:

Literature review: Sources of moisture, precipitation systems, Lagrangian analysis.
Calculating forward/backward trajectories to infer water vapour transport to the Arabian Peninsula.
Identifying trajectories and moisture contributions leading to precipitation events on the Arabian Peninsula.
Obtaining water vapour sources and pathways by Lagranto model using ERA5 reanalysis.
Systematic presentation of findings with comprehensive interpretation of the results.

Skills (not compulsory but preferred or strong motivation to learn):


Assessing Future Changes of Climate Extreme Events in Sudan using CORDEX Ensemble
Supervisors: A. Homoudi1 & K. Barfus2 (Chair of Meteorology)
1 ahmed.homoudi@tu-dresden.de
2 klemens.barfus@tu-dresden.de
